claimed_contribution: The paper shows that metacognitive difficulty can increase evaluations in a specific domain, namely special-occasion products, rather than always decreasing liking.

It proposes and tests that consumers interpret metacognitive experiences through domain-specific lay theories, so the same difficulty cue can imply unfamiliarity or valued exclusivity depending on context.
It offers a more complete account of fluency effects on preference by identifying consumption domain as a boundary condition and mechanism for reversals.
context: The studies examine consumer evaluation of offerings framed as everyday versus special-occasion consumption, including cheese ads, restaurant choices, and chocolate products. The empirical setting is controlled participant studies with student samples.
design_and_data: The paper reports four between-subject experiments. Study 1 (n = 119 undergraduates) manipulated product domain (everyday vs special-occasion cheese) and font readability (easy vs difficult) and measured purchase likelihood, with pretests validating domain and difficulty manipulations. Study 2 (n = 205 students) manipulated domain (casual vs upscale restaurant) and thought-generation difficulty (list 1 vs 5 occasions) and measured willingness to pay. Study 3 (n = 145 female undergraduates) used regular vs difficult font and a source-attribution condition, measured chronic belief that truffles are for special occasions, and tested purchase intent; Study 4 (n = 149 native English-speaking undergraduates) primed everyday vs special concepts, manipulated font difficulty for an ambiguous chocolate ad, and measured evaluation and perceived uniqueness.
main_findings: For everyday products, metacognitive difficulty lowered evaluations, but for special-occasion products, metacognitive difficulty increased evaluations (shown for cheese purchase likelihood and restaurant willingness to pay).
The reversal replicated across different difficulty manipulations, including difficult-to-read fonts and difficult thought generation.

The positive effect of difficulty depended on special-occasion lay theories: it appeared among participants who believed a product was for special occasions or were primed with specialness, was attenuated when difficulty was attributed to font, and operated through greater perceived uniqueness.
one_line_summary: Across four experiments, the paper finds that metacognitive difficulty decreases evaluations of everyday offerings but increases evaluations of special-occasion offerings because difficulty signals uniqueness when exclusivity is valued.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior metacognitive fluency research by showing that difficulty effects are not uniformly negative but depend on consumption-domain lay theories used to interpret the experience.
research_gap: Prior work largely documented that metacognitive difficulty reduces liking by signaling unfamiliarity, but it did not test whether this effect reverses in domains where unfamiliarity or uncommonness carries positive value. The paper addresses this missing domain-specific account by examining special-occasion consumption and the lay theories consumers apply.
research_question: Can metacognitive difficulty increase product evaluation in some consumption domains rather than decrease it? Specifically, does difficulty enhance evaluations of special-occasion products because consumers interpret it as a cue of uniqueness/exclusivity, and do domain-specific lay theories drive this interpretation?
